MarimekkoChart.heroDescription: Free online Marimekko chart maker. Show category share with variable-width stacked columns, compare subcategory mix, and preview the result instantly.
MarimekkoChart.whyItWorksTitle: Show market size and composition in one view.
MarimekkoChart.whyItWorksDescription: A Marimekko chart uses variable-width stacked columns so readers can compare how large each category is and how each category is split across subcategories at the same time.
MarimekkoChart.comparisonPoint1: Column width represents the total share of each category, so the x-axis itself carries business meaning.
MarimekkoChart.comparisonPoint2: Segment height shows the within-category percentage, making mix shifts easy to spot without switching charts.
MarimekkoChart.comparisonPoint3: Use one chart to compare category size, part-to-whole composition, and relative relationships across groups.
MarimekkoChart.productHighlightsTitle: Built for variable-width comparison, live preview, and lightweight editing.
MarimekkoChart.feature1Title: Edit category totals in a table
MarimekkoChart.feature1Description: Update each category label and its three subcategory values directly in the built-in data editor without leaving the chart workflow.
MarimekkoChart.feature2Title: See width and height changes instantly
MarimekkoChart.feature2Description: The preview recalculates both column width and segment height as soon as you change the source values.
MarimekkoChart.feature3Description: Control the chart title, legend labels, and color palette while keeping the output clean and presentation-ready.
MarimekkoChart.feature4Description: Toggle data labels, legend, and grid lines to balance explanation, visual density, and export clarity.
MarimekkoChart.useCasesTitle: Use it for market share, product mix, survey breakdown, and portfolio allocation.
MarimekkoChart.useCase1: Compare regional revenue share while also showing how each region splits across customer segments.
MarimekkoChart.useCase2: Show category size in a retail assortment and the product mix inside each category.
MarimekkoChart.useCase3: Visualize market share by competitor and the channel mix within each competitor's footprint.
MarimekkoChart.useCase4: Summarize survey groups by population size and answer distribution in the same chart.
MarimekkoChart.useCase5: Explain how a total budget is allocated across departments and then split inside each department by spend type.
MarimekkoChart.useCase6: Present portfolio concentration by business unit and the composition of revenue, cost, or risk inside each unit.
MarimekkoChart.decisionGuide1Point1: You need to compare both category size and subcategory composition in one chart.
MarimekkoChart.decisionGuide1Point2: The category totals and the within-category percentages are both important to the story.
MarimekkoChart.decisionGuide1Point3: You want to reveal relationships between groups and the parts that make up each group.
MarimekkoChart.decisionGuide1Point4: Your audience benefits from a compact alternative to separate share and mix charts.
MarimekkoChart.decisionGuide2Point1: You only need simple category comparison and a regular bar chart is easier to scan.
MarimekkoChart.decisionGuide2Point2: You mainly care about within-category composition and a 100% stacked bar chart is clearer.
MarimekkoChart.decisionGuide2Point3: You need precise comparison for many tiny segments and a table or heatmap will be easier to read.
MarimekkoChart.decisionGuide2Point4: You want to show change over time and a line chart or stacked area chart fits better.
MarimekkoChart.faqTitle: Everything people usually ask before building a Marimekko chart.
MarimekkoChart.faq1Answer: A Marimekko chart is a variable-width stacked column chart. Column width shows each category's share of the total, and segment height shows the percentage split inside that category.
MarimekkoChart.faq2Question: What is the difference between a Marimekko chart and a stacked bar chart?
MarimekkoChart.faq2Answer: A stacked bar chart usually uses equal-width columns and focuses on total plus composition. A Marimekko chart also changes the column width, so category size becomes part of the visual encoding.
MarimekkoChart.faq3Question: What kind of data works best in a Marimekko chart?
MarimekkoChart.faq3Answer: It works best with categorical data where each category has a meaningful total and that total can be split into smaller subcategories or segments.
MarimekkoChart.faq4Question: When is a Marimekko chart hard to read?
MarimekkoChart.faq4Answer: It becomes harder to read when you have too many narrow categories, too many tiny subsegments, or an audience that needs exact values rather than pattern comparison.
MarimekkoChart.faq5Question: Is a Marimekko chart the same as a mosaic plot?
MarimekkoChart.faq5Answer: They are closely related. Many people use Marimekko chart, Mekko chart, and mosaic plot interchangeably when describing variable-width category rectangles that encode percentage relationships.
MarimekkoChart.faq6Question: Why use this tool instead of a spreadsheet chart?
MarimekkoChart.faq6Answer: This tool keeps the workflow lightweight: edit category values, preview the variable-width layout instantly, and export a clean chart without building a custom spreadsheet setup.
MarimekkoChart.ctaTitle: Build a Marimekko chart that shows share and mix clearly.
MarimekkoChart.ctaDescription: Enter your category totals and segment values, adjust the labels and colors, and use the live preview above to turn a cross-category percentage story into a chart that is easy to explain.
MarimekkoChart.editorEmptyState: Add at least one category with positive values to render the Marimekko chart.
